title: Azerbaijan: The Land of Fire
subtitle: A gentle introduction to your dad's homeland — a place where the earth itself has quietly burned for thousands of years.

sections.heading: A name that means fire — Odlar Yurdu
sections.body:
Azerbaijanis lovingly call their country Odlar Yurdu, "Land of Fire." It is more than a slogan — it is a name the land earned honestly. In many places across the country, natural gas seeps up through cracks in the ground, and where it meets the open air and a spark, it simply catches fire and keeps burning.

Scholars connect the very name "Azerbaijan" to fire as well. It traces back through many centuries to an ancient governor named Atropates, whose name is understood to mean something like "protected by the (holy) fire." So fire lives in both the ground and the name itself.

This is the world your dad was born into in 1964 — a homeland whose identity, long before oil made it famous, was tied to flame.

sections.heading: The land: mountains, sea, and Baku
sections.body:
Azerbaijan sits at a crossroads, on the boundary where Eastern Europe meets Western Asia. To the north rise the Greater Caucasus Mountains; to the east lies the Caspian Sea, the largest inland body of water on Earth; and the center of the country is broad, warm lowland.

The capital is Baku (Bakı), an ancient and beautiful city on the Caspian shore. It stands on the Abşeron Peninsula, a spit of land reaching into the sea — and it is exactly this peninsula, rich with underground gas and oil, that gives Azerbaijan so many of its natural fires.

Your dad grew up right in this heartland. Sumgait (Sumqayıt), his birthplace, sits on the same Abşeron Peninsula, only about 30 km up the coast from Baku.

sections.heading: Yanar Dağ and the eternal flames
sections.body:
The most famous of Azerbaijan's natural fires is Yanar Dağ, which means "Burning Mountain." On a hillside near Baku, flames rise as high as about three meters straight out of the ground, fed endlessly by gas seeping up through porous sandstone. It has no fuel tank, and no one lights it — it simply burns, and has for as long as anyone remembers.

Such fires once dotted the whole region and astonished travelers for centuries; Marco Polo wrote of the area's strange flames and oils in the 13th century. Today only a few natural fires remain, because so much of the underground gas has been tapped for industry — but Yanar Dağ still burns, and Azerbaijan made it a protected reserve in 2007.

sections.heading: An ancient fire faith: the Atəşgah
sections.body:
Long before Islam, fire was sacred here. Near Baku, in the village of Suraxanı, stands the Atəşgah — a "fire temple" whose very name comes from Persian words meaning "place of fire."

For centuries this spot had a natural eternal flame, and it drew worshippers — including Zoroastrians and, later, Hindu and Sikh pilgrims who traveled the trade routes from India. The temple you can visit today, a small stone complex around a central altar, was built in the 1600s and 1700s.

There is a poignant twist: the natural flame that fed the temple went out in 1969 — five years after your dad was born — as decades of oil and gas extraction drew down the gas beneath the peninsula. The flame there now is kept alive with piped gas, a quiet reminder of how the modern oil age reshaped the ancient landscape.

sections.heading: Black gold: the age of oil
sections.body:
That same underground wealth made Azerbaijan one of the most important places in the history of energy. In 1846, near Baku at Bibiheybət, workers drilled what is widely considered the world's first mechanically drilled oil well — years before the famous early wells in America.

By the late 1800s, Baku was booming. Enterprising families like the Nobels and the Rothschilds poured money in, and by 1899 this one region was producing roughly half of all the oil in the world.

This is the deep root of your dad's early world. His hometown, Sumgait, was built up during the Soviet years specifically as a great chemical and metalworking city, running largely on the oil and gas of the peninsula. To grow up there in the 1960s and 70s was to grow up amid factories, industry, and the hum of the oil economy — the modern face of the Land of Fire.

sections.heading: A modern nation: secular, Muslim, and Turkic
sections.body:
Today Azerbaijan is a modern country that bridges many worlds. Its people are mostly Muslim, yet Azerbaijan is among the most secular Muslim-majority nations anywhere, with a clear separation between religion and public life. The national language, Azerbaijani, is a Turkic language — a close cousin of Turkish — so Azerbaijanis feel kinship with the wider Turkic world while sitting geographically between Europe, Russia, Iran, and the Middle East.

There is real pride in this heritage. In 1918, briefly, Azerbaijan founded the first secular democratic republic in the Muslim world — and in 1919 it granted women the right to vote, ahead of many Western countries. That republic was short-lived, giving way to Soviet rule in 1920, but the memory of it endured as a quiet source of national pride through the Soviet decades your father lived, and it helped shape the independent Azerbaijan that stands today.

terms.en: Land of Fire (Azerbaijan's beloved nickname)
Burning Mountain — a hillside of eternal natural flames
Absheron, the oil- and gas-rich peninsula holding Baku and Sumgait
Fire temple (from Persian: "place of fire")
Sumgait — your dad's home city, a Soviet-era industrial center
Baku, the seaside capital on the Caspian

title: A Soviet Childhood: Your Dad's Sumgait, 1964-1985
subtitle: A gentle walk through the everyday world your father grew up in, from courtyard games to the Caspian shore.

sections.heading: The city he was born into
sections.body:
Your dad arrived in 1964 in Sumgait (Sumqayıt), a young city that had barely existed a generation before him. It grew from a settlement built near Baku in the 1940s and received city status in 1949, then expanded fast as the Soviet Union raised enormous chemical, metallurgical, and aluminium plants along the Caspian coast. By the time your father was a boy, Sumqayıt had become one of the most important industrial centers in the whole USSR — by the late 1980s it was a hub of the Soviet chemical industry, accounting for roughly one-eighth of Azerbaijan's industrial employment.

This matters for understanding him: he grew up in a purpose-built 'workers' city.' The apartment blocks, the schools, the courtyards, the factories — almost everything around him was new, planned, and tied to that industry. It was a place of real pride and opportunity, though the same industrial boom left a heavy pollution legacy that became fully visible only later. His hometown held the Soviet promise and its costs in a single place.

sections.heading: School, the red scarf, and growing up 'prepared'
sections.body:
Soviet childhood came with a clear ladder of belonging that nearly every child climbed, including your dad. The youngest joined the Octobrists (Oktyabryata, roughly ages 7 to 9); around age 10 they became Young Pioneers (Pionerlər), a stage that lasted into the mid-teens; and teenagers from about 14 could join the Komsomol, the Communist youth league. Becoming a Pioneer was a rite of passage — children learned the oath and anthems, then swore in at a solemn ceremony and tied on the famous red neckerchief, whose three corners were said to link Pioneers, Komsomol, and Party.

School itself was rigorous and free, and Soviet Azerbaijan reached near-total literacy. A lovely detail for you: children could study fully in their own language, so your dad could learn in Azerbaijani while taking Russian as a second language. Russian was the door to the wider USSR, and a meaningful share of instruction nationally was conducted in Russian by the late Soviet period. Membership in the youth organizations was formally voluntary but strongly expected; it also opened doors to summer camps, university places, and careers. His generation grew up with the Pioneer motto in their ears: 'Always prepared.'

sections.heading: The həyət and the məhəllə: everyone's kids
sections.body:
If you want to feel your father's daily childhood, picture the courtyard. Families across the USSR lived in apartment blocks arranged around a shared yard — in Russian the dvor, in Azerbaijani the həyət — full of benches, trees, a small playground, and tables where men played nərd (backgammon) and neighbors drank tea. Children poured out after school and were watched, fed, and scolded by every adult in the building. This blended with a much older Azerbaijani tradition: the məhəllə, the tight neighborhood quarter where people genuinely treated one another like one large extended family.

The rhythm of the məhəllə was mutual aid. When someone held a wedding, built a house, or lost a loved one, neighbors simply showed up to help — no invitation needed. For your dad's generation, this was the emotional texture of life: a bit less privacy, but a deep, everyday web of belonging. When your father talks about 'the neighbors,' he may mean something far closer than the word suggests in English.

sections.heading: Factories, queues, and everyday cleverness
sections.body:
Adult life in Sumqayıt revolved around the big plants — synthetic rubber, steel, aluminium, and petrochemicals. Work at a major factory brought a paycheck, but also identity, camaraderie, and often housing and childcare tied to the enterprise. Your dad grew up watching that world of shift-work and factory pride.

The flip side was the deficit — chronic shortages of consumer goods. Even basics like good meat, clothes, shoes, and books could vanish from shelves, and things like bananas, tangerines, or later coffee and caviar were treats you queued for. People stood in one line for meat, another for dairy, then a line for the cashier. So Soviet families became wonderfully resourceful: they relied on blat (connections — knowing 'the right people'), traded favors, grew food at the dacha, mended and remade everything, and celebrated whenever someone 'got hold of' something scarce. This ingenuity is a quiet source of pride for that generation, not only a hardship — building a warm, abundant home life out of a lean system was a genuine skill.

sections.heading: The blue glow of the TV and voices on the radio
sections.body:
Evenings had their own soundtrack. Soviet television was state-run and broadcast across the country in many languages, so your dad's family could catch programming in both Azerbaijani and Russian. There was the evening news program Vremya, popular-science shows, the beloved comedy quiz KVN, music programs, and cartoons that every child of his era can still quote.

The great shared ritual was New Year's Eve — the biggest, warmest holiday of the Soviet year. Families gathered around the set for the New Year 'Little Blue Light' (Goluboy ogonyok) variety show, waited for the Kremlin chimes at midnight, and welcomed the year together. If you ever want a bridge to your father's memories, ask him what was on TV on New Year's Eve — it is a doorway into the feeling of his childhood.

sections.heading: Summers by the Caspian and days at the dacha
sections.body:
Summer is where a Soviet Azerbaijani childhood turned golden. The Absheron Peninsula around Baku and Sumqayıt is dotted with dacha villages — modest summer houses in places like Pirşağı, Şüvəlan, and Mərdəkan — and families would decamp there for weeks. Children played with cousins in the gardens, ate watermelon, and ran down to the Caspian shore. It was fruit trees, grape arbors, barbecues, and endless daylight.

The beaches were part of the Soviet story too. The polished resort at Zağulba served foreign Intourist guests, while ordinary families used simpler public beaches with free umbrellas and showers — plain, but happy. (After the Soviet period, a rising Caspian washed much of that old Intourist beach away.) For your dad's generation, these Caspian summers are often the tenderest memories of all: sun, salt, family, and time. If you can, ask him about the dacha. That is very likely where his happiest childhood stories live.

terms.en: courtyard / shared yard of an apartment block — the heart of daily social life
neighborhood or quarter; a close community treated like extended family
Young Pioneers, the children's organization (roughly ages 10-15)
the Communist youth league for teens and young adults (about 14-28)
Sumgait, the industrial city where your dad was born in 1964
a summer or garden house, often near the Caspian, used for family holidays (from Russian dacha)
backgammon, a classic courtyard and teahouse game

title: Novruz Bayramı: How Azerbaijan Welcomes Spring
subtitle: The warmest, most beloved holiday of your dad's world, where fire, sprouted wheat, and family come together to greet the new year.

sections.heading: What Novruz is, and why it feels so ancient
sections.body:
Novruz Bayramı means "New Day" — it is the Azerbaijani new year, celebrated at the spring equinox, when day and night are equal and winter finally loosens its grip. Depending on the year, it falls around 20–21 March.

What makes it feel timeless is how old it is. Novruz reaches back thousands of years, long before Islam came to the region, into the Zoroastrian world that revered fire as a purifying, sacred force. Azerbaijan has long been known as a land of fire, from the natural flames of the Abşeron peninsula, so this holiday sits very close to the country's soul.

Today it is not really a religious holiday. It belongs to everyone — a celebration of nature waking up, of clearing away the old and welcoming the new. UNESCO lists it as part of humanity's shared intangible cultural heritage.

sections.heading: The four Wednesdays: su, od, yel, torpaq
sections.body:
Novruz doesn't arrive all at once. The four Wednesdays (çərşənbə) leading up to it each honor one of the four elements, and together they tell the story of nature reawakening:

• Su çərşənbəsi (Water) — the snows melt and water begins to move again.
• Od çərşənbəsi (Fire) — fire warms the earth and purifies.
• Yel çərşənbəsi (Wind) — the wind stirs, coaxing the buds open.
• Torpaq çərşənbəsi (Earth) — the soil comes alive, ready to give life.

The last of them, Axır Çərşənbə (the final Wednesday, also called İlaxır Çərşənbə), is the biggest — the real threshold of the holiday, full of bonfires and visiting.

sections.heading: Tonqal: jumping over the fire
sections.body:
The image most people carry of Novruz is the tonqal — a bonfire lit in the yard or the street, with everyone from children to grandparents leaping over the flames.

As they jump (traditionally three times), people call out a little verse: "Ağırlığım, uğurluğum odlara" — roughly, "my burdens and misfortunes, into the fire." The idea is simple and beautiful: you leave the past year's troubles, sickness, and heaviness in the flames and step into spring lighter and renewed. Custom says you shouldn't put the fire out with water — you let it burn down on its own, carrying the year's hardships away with it.

This is exactly the kind of moment your dad may have known as a boy — a warm, slightly mischievous ritual shared with neighbors.

sections.heading: Səməni and the xonça: the holiday table
sections.body:
Weeks ahead, families sprout wheat grains in a dish until they grow into a lush tuft of green called səməni, tied with a red ribbon. It is the heart of the Novruz table — a living symbol of new life and fertility rising from the soil.

The centerpiece is the xonça, a decorated tray. On it sit the səməni, candles (one lit for each family member), dyed eggs, and sweets. Three treats you'll almost always find carry an astral meaning:

• Paxlava — diamond-shaped layered pastry with nuts, said to represent the stars.
• Şəkərbura — a plump, crimped pastry filled with ground nuts and sugar, shaped like the crescent moon.
• Qoğal — a round, flaky bun that stands for the sun.

So the tray itself becomes a little sky. Making these by hand — the whole family crimping şəkərbura together — is half the joy.

sections.heading: Cleaning house, visiting, and small joys
sections.body:
Before Novruz, homes get a deep spring cleaning (təmizlik) — you don't carry old dust, debts, or quarrels into the new year. People make peace with anyone they've fallen out with, buy or sew new clothes, and plant trees.

Then comes the visiting. For several days, families and friends go door to door, drink tea, and share the xonça. Children play egg-tapping — knocking dyed eggs point-to-point to see whose stays uncracked. And there's a shy, sweet custom called papaq atma: kids leave a hat or a bag at a neighbor's door, knock, and hide, and the neighbor fills it with sweets.

Two beloved folk characters, Kosa and Keçəl, turn up in comic street performances, their playful tug-of-war acting out winter losing to spring.

sections.heading: Novruz in your dad's generation
sections.body:
Here is the part that touches your family directly. Your father was born in 1964, in Soviet Azerbaijan. For much of the Soviet period, official attitudes toward Novruz were uneasy — at times it was discouraged as an old religious or nationalist custom, and public celebration was limited.

But it endured. As Azerbaijanis often put it, the holiday could be kept off the streets and off the television, yet not out of people's homes. Many families still lit a candle, sprouted their səməni, and baked their paxlava quietly. For your dad's generation, that could make Novruz feel personal and a little precious — a private keeping-alive of who they were.

With independence in 1991, Novruz returned fully into the open as a proud national holiday. Asking your father what Novruz felt like when he was young — whether it was hushed or joyful, what his mother put on the xonça — may be one of the warmest doors you can open into his world.

terms.en: "New Day" festival — the spring-equinox new year
Wednesday — the four pre-Novruz element-Wednesdays
sprouted wheat grass, a symbol of new life
the decorated holiday tray of sweets, candles, and eggs
the bonfire people jump over for renewal
layered nut pastry (the "stars")
crescent-moon-shaped sweet nut pastry
round bun symbolizing the sun
comic folk characters enacting winter vs. spring

title: The Table: Food & Tea
subtitle: In your dad's world, the surest way to know you're loved is to be fed — and handed a hot glass of tea you can't refuse.

sections.heading: A guest is a gift from God
sections.body:
If you learn one thing about your dad's culture, let it be this: qonaqpərvərlik — hospitality — is close to sacred. An old saying captures it: "Qonaq Allah qonağıdır," a guest is a guest of God. To welcome someone, feed them generously, and send them off well was a matter of honor in every household, extended not only to friends but to strangers and travelers.

For a boy growing up in Sumqayıt in the 1960s and 70s, this was never a rule anyone announced — it was simply the air of the house. A knock at the door meant the kettle went on and the best food came out, whether the family had much or little. When you sit at an Azerbaijani table today, you are stepping into something your father absorbed before he could walk.

sections.heading: Plov, the crown of the table
sections.body:
Plov, a rice pilaf, is the national dish and the centerpiece of any celebration — Azerbaijani cooks are said to prepare more than forty distinct varieties. Long-grain rice is steamed, often turned golden with saffron, and served with a prized bottom crust called qazmaq, made from dough, egg, or thin bread, which turns crisp and buttery. Alongside the rice comes meat, dried fruit, chestnuts, or herbs, depending on the region and the occasion.

Plov was, and still is, the dish for weddings, holidays, and honored guests. In your dad's generation, a great platter of saffron rice carried to the table meant something important was being marked — and that the family was giving you its best.

sections.heading: Dolma, dovğa, kebab, and the təndir
sections.body:
Beyond plov, a few dishes appear again and again. Dolma — tender grape leaves wrapped around minced meat, rice, and onion — is so central that its making and sharing was inscribed on UNESCO's list of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2017. Kebab is seasoned grilled meat, smoky and direct. Dovğa is a warm, tangy yogurt soup thick with rice, chickpeas, and clouds of fresh herbs — mint, dill, coriander — light and deeply comforting.

And there is the bread. Təndir bread is baked against the walls of a clay oven, also called a təndir, coming out with a crackling crust and a soft, faintly tangy inside — perfect with salty white cheese and a mountain of fresh greens, which are eaten by the handful, not sprinkled as garnish. The smell of bread from a təndir is one of those things your father would recognize instantly.

sections.heading: Tea, the everyday ritual
sections.body:
If plov is for celebration, çay (tea) is for every single day. Black tea is served freshly brewed — hot and strong — in the graceful armudu glass, whose name means "pear-shaped." Its narrow waist is practical and poetic at once: it keeps the tea hot at the bottom while the top cools enough to sip, and its silhouette is fondly linked to the figure of a woman, the hostess.

Tea is taken with cubes of sugar, lemon, dried fruit, and small bowls of murabba — whole-fruit preserves of walnut, white cherry, quince, fig, or apricot. One traditional way is to hold a little sweetness on the tongue or the spoon and sip the tea through it. In 2022, UNESCO inscribed the tea culture of Azerbaijan and Türkiye together on its list of Intangible Cultural Heritage, as "a symbol of identity, hospitality and social interaction." Custom holds that you never let a guest leave without at least one glass of tea — so if your dad ever insists you stay for "just one more," now you know the ritual he's honoring.

sections.heading: The çayxana, where the community gathered
sections.body:
Tea also had its own public home: the çayxana, or teahouse. Historically these were gathering places — in your dad's era, largely spaces where men met to talk over the day's news, play backgammon (nərd), and pass hours together over endless glasses of tea. The çayxana was the neighborhood's living room, a hub of conversation and belonging.

This helps explain something quietly powerful about the culture your father comes from: connection wasn't scheduled, it was constant, and much of it flowed through food and tea. To share a meal or a glass of çay was — and is — a way of saying you belong here.

terms.en: hospitality
a guest is a guest of God
pilaf, saffron rice — the national dish
the prized crispy rice crust
stuffed grape leaves
tangy herbed yogurt soup
clay oven (and the bread from it)
tea
pear-shaped tea glass
whole-fruit preserves served with tea
teahouse
backgammon

title: The Musical and Poetic Soul of Azerbaijan
subtitle: The songs and verses that shaped your dad's world — a gentle way to hear the country he was born into.

sections.heading: Muğam — the sound of the soul
sections.body:
If one sound sits at the heart of Azerbaijani music, it is muğam (roughly "moo-GAHM"). It is a classical, deeply emotional art of singing and playing built on improvisation: musicians follow ancient modes and moods but shape each performance in the moment, a little like the way jazz grew out of the blues. The word itself comes from the Arabic maqām, meaning a mode or "station" — a term that carries a spiritual echo of the soul's ascent, which is part of why muğam is felt as such reverent, searching music.

A muğam performance is usually a trio led by a singer called the xanəndə, who carries and decorates the melody with sighs, ornaments and long emotional lines, joined by two instrumentalists. The songs speak of love, longing, faith and the meaning of life, and you can still hear muğam at weddings and festivals today.

UNESCO inscribed Azerbaijani muğam on its list of the intangible cultural heritage of humanity in 2008. A short muğam piece even travels aboard the Voyager spacecraft's Golden Record — a fragment of Azerbaijan sent out among the stars.

sections.heading: The three voices: tar, kamança and balaban
sections.body:
Three instruments carry the soul of this music, and each has its own personality.

• The tar is a long-necked lute with a double-bowl, figure-eight body held against the chest. It carries both the main melody and a droning undertone, and it is so beloved that UNESCO honored its craftsmanship and playing in 2012.

• The kamança (also spelled kamancha) is a bowed spike-fiddle held upright on the knee. Its voice "answers" and echoes the singer, almost like a second person in the conversation. UNESCO recognized it in 2017.

• The balaban is a soft, double-reed wind instrument — closer to an oboe than a flute — with a warm, almost human, mournful tone. It is the very sound chosen for the Voyager recording, and its craft and playing were added to UNESCO's list in 2023.

When your dad was a boy in the 1960s and 70s, these instruments would have been the everyday soundtrack of celebrations, radio broadcasts and family gatherings.

sections.heading: Aşıq bards and street-corner meyxana
sections.body:
Alongside the refined world of muğam lives an older, more rustic tradition: the aşıq ("ah-SHUHK"), a travelling bard who is poet, singer and storyteller all at once. Accompanying himself on a lute called the saz, the aşıq weaves poetry, music, drama and dance into long epic tales called dastan — stories of love, heroism and wisdom passed down through generations. UNESCO inscribed the art of the Azerbaijani aşıq in 2009. Think of them as the country's keepers of memory, much like folk balladeers elsewhere.

There is also a livelier, everyday cousin: meyxana, a fast, witty, improvised rhyming performance over a drumbeat, often with several people trading clever verses back and forth. It is the playful, street-smart side of Azerbaijan's poetic instinct, and it remains hugely popular. Together these traditions show how, in your dad's generation, poetry wasn't only for books — it was spoken, sung and improvised out loud.

sections.heading: Üzeyir Hacıbəyov — the father of a modern sound
sections.body:
One name towers over Azerbaijani music: Üzeyir Hacıbəyov (1885–1948), often called the father of Azerbaijani classical music. His guiding idea was to marry the improvised soul of muğam with the grand forms of European opera and orchestra.

In 1908 he premiered Leyli and Majnun in Baku, widely regarded as the first opera of the Muslim East, its story drawn from the classic poem by Füzuli. In 1937 came his heroic opera Koroğlu ("the blind man's son"), one of the most cherished works in the national repertoire. He also composed the music for Azerbaijan's national anthem, first used by the independent republic of 1918–1920 and readopted after the country regained independence in 1991. For your dad's generation, growing up in Soviet Azerbaijan, Hacıbəyov was a bridge between the old world and the modern concert hall — and a lasting source of national pride.

sections.heading: The beloved voices your dad grew up with
sections.body:
The decades your dad grew up in produced singers whose voices are still treasured across generations. A few names he would surely know:

• Rəşid Behbudov (1915–1989), called "the golden voice of Azerbaijan," whose warm, romantic style carried him on tours far and wide, from Iran and Turkey to India, Japan and Latin America.

• Müslüm Maqomayev (1942–2008), a magnetic baritone and matinee-idol figure adored across the entire Soviet Union, who blended opera with popular song.

• Zeynəb Xanlarova (born 1936), a celebrated soprano honored as a People's Artist of the USSR, known for singing beautifully in many languages.

And bridging the classical and modern eras is Alim Qasımov (born 1957), a living master of muğam whom the International Music Council and UNESCO honored with their Music Prize in 1999 — proof that this ancient art is still very much alive. Asking your dad which of these singers filled his childhood home could be a warm way into his memories.

sections.heading: Nizami and Füzuli — poetry older than the songs
sections.body:
Beneath all this music lies a poetic tradition many centuries old, and two names stand at its summit.

Nizami Gəncəvi (c. 1141–1209), long associated with the city of Gəncə where he lived and died, is celebrated as one of the greatest romantic epic poets of the medieval world. His masterpiece is the Xəmsə (the "Quintet" or "Five Treasures"), five long poems that include Khosrow and Shirin, Layla and Majnun, and The Seven Beauties — works that shaped literature from Anatolia to India for centuries.

Füzuli (c. 1494–1556) wrote in Azerbaijani Turkish and gave the tragic love story of Leyli and Majnun its most beloved form — the very poem that later became Hacıbəyov's first opera. So the thread runs unbroken: a 16th-century poet's verses became a 1908 opera your dad's grandparents might have heard, sung in a style honored by UNESCO in his own lifetime. That continuity — poetry becoming music becoming shared pride — is the heartbeat of the culture you're reconnecting with.

terms.en: classical, improvised Azerbaijani art music (UNESCO heritage)
the lead singer in a muğam ensemble
long-necked lute, the melodic heart of muğam
upright bowed spike-fiddle
soft double-reed wind instrument (oboe-like)
travelling bard-poet who plays the saz lute
fast, witty improvised rhyming performance
Nizami's "Quintet" of five epic poems

title: The Language & Its Alphabets
subtitle: Your dad's mother tongue is a warm, musical cousin of Turkish, and the alphabet used to write it changed several times during the last century, which is why he likely still reads Cyrillic most comfortably.

sections.heading: A Turkic language, cousin to Turkish
sections.body:
Azerbaijani (in the language itself, Azərbaycan dili) is a Turkic language, part of the same family as Turkish, Turkmen, and others. More specifically it belongs to the Oghuz branch, the western group of Turkic languages, which is why it sits so close to Turkish.

How close? Speakers of Azerbaijani and Turkish can often understand a good deal of one another with only moderate exposure, something linguists call "mutual intelligibility." The two are not identical (vocabulary, some sounds, and grammar differ), but they feel like close relatives rather than strangers. If you ever notice your dad following a Turkish TV channel or song with ease, this is why.

sections.heading: An alphabet that kept changing
sections.body:
Here is the remarkable part, and it helps explain a lot about your dad's world. Within a single century, Azerbaijani was written in three different scripts, with the Latin script used twice:

• Perso-Arabic script, the traditional writing, used for centuries up into the 1920s.
• Latin script, officially adopted in 1929 (replacing Arabic) and reformed in 1933.
• Cyrillic script, introduced in 1939-1940 under the Soviet Union.
• Latin script again, brought back after independence in 1991.

So the language stayed the same, but the letters on the page were reinvented again and again. Very few languages in the world have been through this.

sections.heading: Why Cyrillic, and why it matters for your dad
sections.body:
While Azerbaijan was part of the Soviet Union, the authorities directed that the language be written in the Cyrillic alphabet, the same family of letters used for Russian. This shift happened in 1939-1940. Historians generally describe one motive as political: to loosen the cultural ties between Soviet Azerbaijanis and neighboring Turkey, which by then used the Latin script.

This is the key personal point. Your dad was born in 1964 in Sumqayıt and went through Soviet schooling. That means he learned to read and write his own language in Cyrillic. Throughout his childhood, his education, and his early working life, Cyrillic Azerbaijani was simply "normal" writing. Those characters were in everyday use for roughly half a century.

sections.heading: 1991: independence, and the return to Latin
sections.body:
As the Soviet Union came apart, Azerbaijan restored its independence, declared on 18 October 1991 and confirmed by a nationwide referendum that December, shortly before the USSR itself formally ended on 26 December 1991. (The years that followed were not easy, including the conflict over Nagorno-Karabakh, but that is a separate story.)

One of the new parliament's early acts was to bring back a Latin-based alphabet, reversing the Soviet-era switch. There followed a transition period through the 1990s in which both scripts were accepted. The change was completed everywhere from 1 August 2001, under a presidential decree, after which official and public writing used Latin letters. That date is now marked each year as the Day of the Azerbaijani Alphabet and Language.

sections.heading: What this means when you connect with your dad
sections.body:
Because he was schooled entirely in the Soviet era, your father's generation learned Cyrillic first and most deeply. The full switch to Latin came when he was already a grown man (around 27 at independence, and 37 when the change was completed in 2001). Reports at the time noted that older Azerbaijanis often struggled with the new Latin road signs and print.

So do not be surprised if your dad reads and writes Azerbaijani more comfortably in Cyrillic than in today's Latin script; a handwritten note from him may even come in Cyrillic. It is not a quirk. It is a whole century of history showing up in his handwriting. If you ever want to read something in his hand, learning to sound out Azerbaijani Cyrillic could be a beautiful bridge between you.

sections.heading: A note on the letters
sections.body: Modern (Latin) Azerbaijani has a few special letters worth knowing, because they carry sounds English does not spell the same way: ə (a short "a" roughly as in "cat"), ı (an "uh"-like vowel with no dot), ö and ü (rounded vowels, as in German), ç ("ch"), ş ("sh"), ğ (a soft, throaty g), x (a raspy "kh"), and q (a hard, g-like sound). Even the country's own name shows several of them at once: Azərbaycan.
